In each iteration, Algorithm 2.1 first forms the new basis vector v through
k+1
a matrix-vector product with A (line 5), which has a cost of O(N), assuming that
A is sparse with N nonzero entries. To orthogonalize v against the previous k
k+1
basis vectors, the modified Gram-Schmidt process (lines 6-9) requires an additional
O(mn) operations. As this process repeated m times, the total cost of Algorithm 2.1
is O(nm2+N).
If the matrix A is Hermitian, we can use the Lanczos iteration [29] to generate
V and H using a short-term recurrence, reducing the total orthogonalization cost
m m
to O(mn). However, to evaluate (2.3), the program needs to store the full basis V

regardless if A is Hermitian or not.
